Welcome to the Gary Halbert Letter All-Star Audio series. I'm Kevin Rogers. Ed Dale has been educating people about ways to harness the power of the internet to grow their businesses for longer than just about anyone. In fact, it was Ed who, after hiring Gary to write and consult on some projects, finally convinced the doubting Halbert there was something real and valid afoot on the then flickering light of the web. Of course, we now know that direct response marketing and the internet are enjoying a thriving romance that only grows stronger every day. Ed Dale continues to teach and pioneer through his writings on his blog and the insightful audio sessions he records while out walking his dog called Walking with Larry. Here's Ed Dale reading the Halbert classic Do It by the Numbers. Way west of Jew Fish Creek. Dear friend, there I was at 15,000 feet, both engines on fire and my parachute in the laundry. You ever feel like that? You've got a marketing emergency on your hands and you've got to solve it right now? Listen, I get calls nearly every day from people who need instant marketing solutions. They're not copywriters and they have no advertising experience. They don't trust ad agencies and rightly so. And they haven't the luxury of enough time to become grounded in marketing theory by reading Robert Collier, Claude Hopkins and so on. In fact, they have no desire to become marketing experts. No, it's just that every expert they've ever hired has disappointed them and since I'm usually too booked up to do any new jobs on a right now basis, they want to know what should I do? Do it by numbers, I tell them. Here's what I mean. Look, if you have a good product or service, you probably already know exactly how to sell it when you were talking to someone during a one-on-one situation. Whether you are talking to someone in person or perhaps on the telephone, it probably doesn't matter. My bet is you know exactly what words to use and what buttons to push to most impress your prospective customer. Am I right? I thought so. However, here is a curious fact. Most people who are very good in a personal selling situation freeze up when it comes to transferring their sales pitch into the printed word or else they struggle to get creative. Forget that rubbish. Here's a better way. The first thing you do is you begin to record with the other party's permission all of your conversations whenever you are talking to one of your prospects on the phone. Keep doing this until you are able to forget you are doing it. Why? The reason is simple. You see, at first, when you are aware that what you are saying is being recorded, you will stifle yourself. You'll try to make sure what you are saying is grammatically correct or you'll try to be clever or more dramatic or less dramatic or whatever. But if you keep doing it long enough, you'll eventually forget about the damn tape recorder and you'll revert to being your own natural, wonderful self and you'll deliver your usual compelling sales presentation. And hopefully after a while, we'll have a recording of you talking to a prospect when you are at concert pitch. Goodie. But what's next? This. What we do next is take that recording to a typist and have it transcribed. Then, after it has been transcribed, we take a copy of that typed transcription and number each one of the paragraphs. By the way, for our purposes, a paragraph is one or more sentences that express a single idea or aspect of a subject. Okay. Now, let's say we have the first draft of our sales pitch in print and it contains 47 paragraphs and you have duly numbered them one through 47. Next, we carefully go over each one of these paragraphs and give each of them another number that is based on a scale of one to 10. 10 being the highest. What are we doing here is ranking each paragraph as to how much favorable influence that paragraph has on our prospect. For example, let's say we are pitching a 31 foot Bertram sports fishing boat, one of the best. And we know from previous experience that our customers are very interested in the fact that the boat has a wide beam and a whole configuration that makes it extremely seaworthy and very interested in the fact that it comes with matching curtains and towels. So let's say the paragraphs about the seaworthiness of the boat get a rank of eight and the one about matching curtains and towels gets a ranking of two. Whatever. So what we've got now is a sales pitch in print. That is 47 paragraphs long. And we're in each paragraph has been rated for wonderfulness on a scale of one to 10. Now, what I want you to do next is cut out all of those paragraphs with a pair of scissors and divide them into three groups. The first group will consist of those paragraphs that describe the benefits of owning a Bertram and the second group will consist of those paragraphs that tell interesting facts about the boat. The third group will be those paragraphs that don't say anything about the Bertram or else paragraphs that do not advance your sales presentation in any way whatsoever. Now, throw the third group away and arrange the other two groups in rank from 10 on down. Next, throw out all the paragraphs with a rating of five or less. Next, rewrite the remaining paragraphs. But beware. Don't change them much. Just more or less clean them up. Take out the urs and arms, the redundant words and sentences and sort of tighten up your sentence structure and grammar. Let us press on. What I'd like you to do now is try to forget you are trying to write an ad or a sales letter. Instead, what I want you to do is concentrate on writing a memo. A long memo. Write it to someone you can easily visualize in your mind's eye. Someone who is a good prospect for a good, relatively cheap sports fishing boat. Someone extremely good looking and modest. Someone like me, Sir Gary of Halbert, Ace of Space, Prince of Print, Count of Cable and all around Bon Vivant. Your memo should read something like this. Memo. Dear Gary, I hear you are looking for a good, seaworthy sports fishing boat. Listen, I've got a good one for sale. It's a really a humdinger. Here are seven interesting facts about this boat. One. Insert highest ranked interesting fact. Two. Insert second highest ranked fact. Three. Insert third highest ranked fact. Four. You've got the idea, right? Five. Ditto. Six, etc. Seven and so forth. Hey Gary, what about those apples? Maybe now you can see why this particular boat is such a honey. There are many benefits to owning a boat like this. Here are five of the main ones. One. Insert paragraph on highest ranked benefits of owning this boat. Two. Insert second highest ranked paragraph. Three. Ditto. Four, etc. Five and so forth. Well, anyway, Gary, those are just a few of the highlights about the wonderfulness of this boat. But actually, there are many more reasons it makes sense for you to own a vessel like this. And I'd love to explain them to you in person. So here's the deal. I know you're busy. What with all your clients and writing your newsletter and scuba diving with Paulette and so forth. So I'm going to give you a chance to digest this memo and then in a few days I'll give you a call and see if you're interested. In the meantime, if you want more info sooner, you can call me at 000 000 000 000 000. By the way, I love your newsletter and I really enjoy reading it every month in spite of your conceited ego maniacal ramblings. Sincerely, Joe subscriber and astute student. Now listen, I want to know something. It is this. Did you think that little lesson was a bit too simple? If so, you were doing yourself a great disservice. You see, if you can grasp the essence of what I've just imparted, you will have learned an enormously important marketing lesson. Here is that lesson. Stop worrying about those awards you'd like to win. Hark unto me. Your job is not to be creative, clever or win awards. No, your job is to sell. Damn it. Selling is not yelling. Selling is not tricks. Selling is an impressive, forceful communication of facts and benefits. What I have just taught you is a far cry from being the end all and be all of how to create advertising. However, that simple little process of recording your person to person sales presentations and then transferring them to paper as I've outlined works far better than trying to dream up some award winning vapidness. Check this out. There's a guy named Bruce Harovitz who writes a column on marketing which appears in the business section of the Los Angeles Times every Tuesday. Recently, on June 9th, one of his articles was about a gentleman named Sean Kevin Fitzpatrick who is an executive vice president and creative director of an ad firm named Campbell Uelco. The article is about Fitzpatrick's hard beat of America commercial which consists mostly of a catchy song that he created for the Chevrolet Division of General Motors. The article tells how successful this campaign is and how Chevrolet's current series of car and truck ads have been nominated for 10 Clio Awards. It's a stupid commercial. In fact, this campaign is so stupid it would appear to me that Fitzpatrick doesn't even know what advertising is or at least what it's supposed to be. Advertising is not to remind someone of your existence. It is not songwriting, dancing, bears or tricky words and catchy music. No, good advertising is simply salesmanship multiplied. I don't know how much money has been wasted on this moronic campaign. However, I do know that according to this same article that during the first quarter of 1987 Chevrolet sales are down 12.1% compared to what they were last year. Is it any wonder? But you know what? People who can't produce results often get very creative when it comes to creating cop outs. And in the ad business, if you can't produce sales, what you do is you tell your client that you are building his image. You can tell him how all this money he's spending will pay off sometime in years to come. Listen to this quote from another advertising expert in the same article. But this campaign can't be measured in short term results. It's an image building campaign. Image is the key issue in the car market. Blah blah blah. What garbage? You know what Fitzpatrick should have done instead of writing a song? It's simple. He should have found out who the 10 best Chevy salesmen are in the entire US. Then he should have met with these men and watched them sell cars. And he should have recorded their sales presentations and taken notes. And then he should have created an intelligent campaign built around the best parts of those effective sales presentations. Maybe this guy deserves to win a Grammy, but as sure as hell shouldn't get any awards based on his ability to create advertising that sells cars. Oh well. I suppose if it wasn't for guys like him, there wouldn't be so much demand for guys like me. Onward. Look, I'm coming to the close of this month's letter. And before I do, I want to tell you about a couple of things that are not directly related to marketing. I hope you'll bear with me. I'm going to tell you about one of these items because it is so potentially important. And the other because it is so utterly delightful. Item one. Back in the 70s, someone told me about a doctor who became wealthy developing some industrial patents that were used in Europe. Apparently he used his wealth to set up a clinic in Paris to treat patients and do research. However, when the Nazis invaded Paris, he found it necessary to flee to the US. When he got here, he set up another clinic and began treating cancer patients and doing more research. Anyway, I met this man sometime in 1971 or 1972 and I was very impressed with him. He showed me a book he had published with remarkable before and after pictures of patients he'd cured of cancer. In fact, he autographed the book for me and he also showed me letters from some of the authorities in Establishment Medicine that said, in effect, that although his ideas may have some merit, any real cure for cancer will come from the establishment. What this doctor told me is that most MDs and hospitals don't want to treat cancer patients the way he does because it is too expensive and time consuming. Now let's flash forward to a few days ago. I'm sitting in my office when I get a call from one of my clients, an international publisher, who says, Gary, I'm thinking about doing a book on this doctor in Manhattan. He's 91 years old and he's been treating AIDS patients with a 100% success rate. Listen, I said, is this guy's name Ravici? Well, guess what? It appears it's the same doctor I met. Now hold on, I can't vouch for the truth of what you have just read and it is very cruel to hold out false hope for people who are seriously ill. However, I've heard enough about this doctor from enough resources that, if you know someone with AIDS, you might suggest they check this guy out. He runs the Institute of Applied Biology in Manhattan and the number is 212-976-9669. Once again, I'm not saying all this is true. I'm just saying it seems worthy of investigation. Item 2. I had dinner last Saturday with an old scuba buddy of mine and he introduced me to an incredible restaurant that specializes in Southern cooking. This restaurant is called Aunt Kizzy's Back Porch and it is owned by a black couple named Adolf and Mary Doolan. Let me tell you, Adolf Doolan absolutely bends over backwards to see that his guests are happy. It is a real joy to see a man with this much grace and caring and the food, it is quite simply the best I've ever eaten in any restaurant anywhere in the world. Period. By the way, don't look for fancy decor. Aunt Kizzy's is located at 4371 Glencoe Avenue which is in the Villa Marina Shopping Centre. Sincerely, Gary C. Halbert. The very most semi-handsome Ad-Man who ever lived. P.S. Someone once said that if all my newspaper ads were being run by one company instead of separate clients, that that company would be the third largest newspaper advertiser in the US. I don't know if that statement was true back when it was uttered but I sure as hell have created a lot of newspaper ads that have produced phenomenal results. Want to know some of my secrets? Tune in next month. I daresay Bruce Horowitz won't have revealed any of them by then. Wow, I missed that bugger so much. It's funny when I type in autocorrect into my iPhone, you know, occasionally, no sex Gary at aol.com will pop up. And I remember him being so tickled about that email address. He loved it. I was thrilled, proud, honoured to call Gary a friend and, you know, more importantly for me and my family, a mentor. I actually went to stay and live with Gary for a while and hired him exclusively for six months at one point. And it was the best money that I ever spent. I learned so much from him and reading back that letter now, that combination of incredibly solid advice, a brilliant method of writing a letter which would stack up today with you doing a letter, a video, a podcast, or any of the things that Gary would initially be very, very circumstance. I don't know what to expect of, but came around. You know, I was had the honour, I suppose, of convincing him that this Internet thing was really worth something. And when he took to the Internet and he figured that out, he's applying fundamentals of salesmanship really did refine things. And in some ways he was way ahead of his time. I remember we put a phone number on a sales letter and it worked really well in this particular market. And when he talked about this in a copywriting forum on a webinar, you know, he was howled down people, you know, were outright. Why would you put a phone number on a Internet website? Why would you do that when you can take an order on the spot? And Gary argued that there are a lot of people who don't feel comfortable doing that. And of course, what do we see today? More and more and more websites that are spending hundreds of thousands, if not millions of dollars in pay per click. The number one goal was to get you on the phone with a dirty big phone number on the website. He bitickled pink, I think now at how science and the study of the brain has caught up to stuff that he always considered to be, you know, just common sense. And some things that he intuited. And I think it was Ben Franklin who said common sense is most uncommon. Well, Halbert was just a person to have that common and common good sense. By spending that time with Gary so closely, there are a few things that I wanted to just take a moment to pass on that I learned. Not the techniques like the technique that he described just then. And he literally did that. I was with him. We would literally have three by five cars and he'd spread them all over his apartment trying to figure out what are the most interesting wow facts as he used to call them. So he practiced very much what he preached. But there is certain elements to the way that he worked, which again, speaking here, have been proved. He scientifically proved work. You know, things like never stop writing. When he was writing copy, he would write and his pen would never leave that page. It would never stop working on that yellow legal pad. And it's funny, I would read some of this stuff. I remember one very vivid example where he was writing a letter for a putter, you know, a golf putter. And he'd written something along the lines of and the very special metal on the putter head is made out of the foreskin of an albino baby seal. I said, Gary, what's this? And he said, well, I'll pick that up in editing, won't I? And I don't have the time to stop and figure out what the actual metal is. It'll be made of something, right? And he was so true. And if you analyze the best writers than the most productive writers, whether it be fiction or nonfiction, they all share the same characteristic, whereas an amateur like you or I would stop and go and look up that information, lose their train of thought, and then lose their entire focus and blow an entire copywriting session. He never edited. He never spell checked. He never did any of those things when he was creating. He separated the creative phase from the editing phase. And after he'd created, he'd always put that sales letter away and he'd sit on it for a day or two. I remember, you know, he'd be stuck and he'd go on these drives and we'd drive down to the keys or drive up the coast in Florida. And it turns out these mental breaks again have been scientifically proven now to be very crucial to the creative process to get some distance between your first draft and when you're ready to edit. To this day, I still write a blog post. Leave it overnight before I edit it just simply because of what I'd learned from Gary. Then he did something which was quite remarkable to observe. Once he had a draft of his sales letter that he felt you would read correctly. And you know what? And I don't want to sky it here, but it's a credit to Gary's copy that I flubbed in 2481 words, three lines. And that's a credit to the way Gary wrote his copy because he would always read it out loud. And if it didn't read well, if he had to stumble or stop, then he knew that the sentence needed cleaning up and he'd have another go at it and make it smoother and cleaner. Another incredible technique, one that he did religiously. The other thing that he would do, and this is what I was about to tell you about, was that he would pick up the phone and I would remember he'd call Dean Jackson or Scott or John Carlton and it'd be like, Dean. He would do this and he would start reading the sales letter and just like you heard me read in 16 odd minutes that letter, he would read it out and then at the end of it he'd say, what do you think? And he'd get his feedback. He was religious about getting feedback and it's funny because you think the prints of print would be above feedback. But my experience and the experience of watching Gary is that the better the person is, the more feedback they seek because that's how you really improve something and how you polish and buff something into an incredibly polished gem. So they were things to me that with my time with Gary, I'd remember all of those lessons. You know, and just reading back that letter today, which was such a privilege and I thank Bond and Kevin for the opportunity because the copy was so approachable. It was so funny and it didn't appeal to everybody. Not everybody loved his style. In fact, some people hated his style and he knew that. But he also knew he only wanted to work with people that got his style and let's face it as one of the most effective copywriters the 20th century ever saw. He had some basis of evidence for that. It was a privilege to be able to work with Gary Halbert and the lessons that we can learn today, his letters, that newsletter I just read, that process stacks up today. Perhaps even more so today than it ever did in any decade, whether it be the 70s, the 80s, when he wrote that, when the AIDS epidemic was at its most fearful. 90s, 2000s, all the 10s. It was a privilege to work with Gary and he has so much to teach you even though he's passed on all these years. Thank you, NoSexGarryatAOL.com. My family and I wouldn't be where we are unless it was for you and the stuff that I learned from you. Thanks. Thanks, mate. Really appreciate it. Thank you.
